figures.gloss: The supreme of the Limbu Mundhum — a formless, omniscient power, 'she who knows all', from whom everything issues. Too vast for direct approach, she is worshipped in her descended, graspable manifestation as the grandmother goddess Yuma Sammang.
figures.facet: A supreme conceived as knowing rather than ruling: her name centres omniscience, and her theology solves the remote-high-god problem by folding the distant absolute and the intimate hearth deity into one being at two intensities.
figures.provenance: I.S. Chemjong, 'History and Culture of the Kirat People' (1966); J.R. Subba's studies of Limbu religion (Yumaism).

figures.gloss: The 'grandmother' — the principal deity of Limbu household worship and the earthly form of Tagera Ningwaphuma. She guards hearth, harvest and lineage, and receives the first fruits of the year at the Chasok Tangnam harvest rite before anyone may eat the new grain.
figures.facet: The supreme worshipped as kin: addressing the highest power as 'grandmother' domesticates transcendence into the household's own generational structure.
figures.provenance: Philippe Sagant, 'The Dozing Shaman: The Limbus of Eastern Nepal' (1996); I.S. Chemjong (1966).

figures.gloss: The oral scripture of the Kirati peoples — cosmogony, origin-genealogies, migration routes and ritual procedure, recited in a ritual register distinct from everyday speech (Limbu mundhum; Rai muddum/ridum). It is performed, not read: Chemjong's 20th-c. compilations put parts in writing, but authority still lives in correct recitation.
figures.facet: A scripture that is a verb as much as a corpus: reciting the Mundhum's routes and genealogies is itself the efficacious ritual act, so the text and the rite are the same event.
figures.provenance: I.S. Chemjong, 'Kirat Mundhum' (1961); Martin Gaenszle, 'Origins and Migrations: Kinship, Mythology and Ethnic Identity among the Mewahang Rai' (2000).

figures.gloss: The primal couple of the Rai (Khambu) mundhum: Paruhang of the sky and Sumnima of the earth, whose courtship, separation and reunion generate the beings of the world. They are honoured in the biannual Sakela festival, whose sili dance-steps mimic the movements of animals and the labour of cultivation.
figures.facet: The world-parents pattern kept in active liturgical use: the primal marriage is not archived myth but the explicit referent of a living agricultural festival cycle (Sakela Ubhauli and Udhauli).
figures.provenance: Martin Gaenszle (2000); Rai Kirat ethnography of the Sakela festival.

figures.gloss: The Kirati ritual specialists — phedangma, samba and yeba/yema among the Limbu, bijuwa and nakchhong among the Rai — who recite the Mundhum, retrieve strayed souls, and in death rites guide the deceased back along the chanted ancestral migration route to the land of the forebears.
figures.facet: Soul-geography: the dead are ritually walked backwards up the migration path, so the Mundhum's route-recitations double as a map, and every funeral rehearses the people's remembered history in reverse.
figures.provenance: Martin Gaenszle (2000) on Mewahang Rai death ritual; Philippe Sagant (1996) on Limbu specialists.

figures.gloss: Limbu teacher and martyr (1704-1741) who revived the Sirijunga script — attributed by tradition to a 9th-c. royal namesake — transcribed Mundhum material, and taught Limbu literacy across Limbuwan and Sikkim. Seen as a threat to Sikkim's Buddhist order, he was seized and killed by Tashiding-associated monks in 1741.
figures.facet: Script revival as religious resistance, two centuries early: his martyrdom made alphabet, scripture and ethnic identity a single cause, the template later Kirat and Northeast-Indian revivals would repeat.
figures.provenance: I.S. Chemjong (1966); Limbu script scholarship (history of the Sirijunga script).

figures.gloss: Limbu reformer-saint (1885-1949) who preached a purified Mundhum practice — vegetarianism, sobriety, congregational worship, and Limbu-script literacy — founding the Satyahangma reform movement; declared a national luminary of Nepal in 2009.
figures.facet: The single most consequential Kirat religious figure of the modern era: it is his 20th-century reform, not the ancient Mundhum substrate alone, that gave 'Kirat' the organized, congregational shape that let it register as a named religion on Nepal's census.
figures.provenance: I.S. Chemjong, 'History and Culture of the Kirat People' (1966); J.R. Subba's studies of Limbu religion (Yumaism).

figures.gloss: The legendary first king of the Kirat dynasty of the Kathmandu valley, named in the chronicle tradition (Gopal Rajvamshavali) as the founding sovereign of Kirat rule.
figures.facet: The charter figure a contested antiquity rides on: the tradition's own claim to a documented ancient 'Kirata' lineage is carried, in practice, by Yalambar's legendary kingship rather than by unbroken historical record — legend doing the work continuity cannot yet document.
figures.provenance: The Gopal Rajvamshavali chronicle tradition; I.S. Chemjong (1966), carried as dynastic legend rather than verified history.

figures.gloss: The orphan boy of the Mewahang Rai origin cycle whose abandonment, survival, and eventual recovery of ancestral knowledge found the ritual order that the muddum recites.
figures.facet: A culture hero built from want rather than gift: where many culture heroes steal fire or tools from the gods, Hetchakuppa's founding act is simply surviving abandonment long enough to recover what the ancestors already knew — the ritual order rescued rather than invented.
figures.provenance: Martin Gaenszle, 'Origins and Migrations: Kinship, Mythology and Ethnic Identity among the Mewahang Rai' (2000) — the roster's own key Rai source.

figures.gloss: The vital force or tutelary power of the Limbu household head, ritually 'raised' in periodic household rites on which the house's strength, luck, and social standing are held to depend.
figures.facet: Status made a maintainable substance: Nahangma is not a fixed rank but a power that must be periodically re-raised through ritual, tying a household head's standing to ongoing ritual labor rather than to birth alone.
figures.provenance: Philippe Sagant, 'The Dozing Shaman: The Limbus of Eastern Nepal' (1996) — the roster's own Limbu ethnographic source.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: the formless omniscient supreme and her worshippable manifestation as the household grandmother — one deity at two intensities.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the soul-travelling ritual specialist as the community's working core — recitation-led soul retrieval and guidance of the dead; typological kinship only, no demonstrated descent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a sky-power paired with an earth-mother counterpart (as Tengri with Umai) — the Inner-Asian sky-frame in Himalayan dress; structural analogy, not a proven lineage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: guiding the dead along a recited route through a dangerous landscape — a shared Himalayan death-ritual grammar between Kirati funerals and the Bon death-passage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: priestly authority carried in memorized recitation-craft rather than in books — the Mundhum beside Bon's drung and deu; Himalayan typological kinship, kept at analogy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: script revival as religious resistance — the 18th-c. Limbu martyr and the 20th-c. Meitei revivalist each made an alphabet the vessel of a suppressed religion's return.

Two centuries apart within the same Limbu corpus: Sirijunga's 18th-century script-and-literacy martyrdom and Falgunanda's 20th-century Satyahangma reform both make Limbu-script literacy the vehicle of religious renewal.
Hetchakuppa's origin cycle is itself muddum material — a central narrative the Rai recitation-tradition carries, giving the Rai side of the roster a mythic hero to set beside the Limbu-heavy file.

Two 19th/20th-century South Asian reformer-founders who organized a congregational, purificatory, literacy-driven religious movement out of an older substrate — Falgunanda's Satyahangma beside the Brahmo Samaj's reformer-founder office, structural kinship without contact.
Nahangma is a close-to-textbook instance of the vital-force joint itself: an impersonal, accumulable, ritually renewable power (mana-like) vested in a household head rather than a deity.

dossier.essence: The ancestral religion of the Kirati peoples of the eastern Nepal hills — above all the Limbu and the Rai (Khambu), with related Yakkha and Sunuwar traditions — extending into Sikkim and Darjeeling, and grounded in the Mundhum, the oral corpus of cosmogony, genealogy, migration routes and ritual procedure. 'Kirat' has been a recognized census religion of Nepal since 1991; the name 'Kirata' appears in late-Vedic and epic Sanskrit sources, though the equation with the modern Kirati peoples is contested.
dossier.cosmos: In the Limbu Mundhum everything issues from Tagera Ningwaphuma, the formless omniscient supreme, 'she who knows all', worshipped in her graspable descended form as the grandmother goddess Yuma Sammang; the Rai mundhum tells instead of the primal couple Paruhang of the sky and Sumnima of the earth, whose courtship and reunion generate the world's beings. The remembered migration of the ancestors structures sacred geography: at death the soul must be guided back along that chanted route to the land of the forebears, so every funeral rehearses the people's history in reverse.
dossier.practice: Ritual specialists — phedangma, samba and yeba/yema among the Limbu, bijuwa and nakchhong among the Rai — recite the Mundhum in a ritual register distinct from everyday speech, retrieve strayed souls, heal, and conduct the death rites that walk the deceased home. The household worships Yuma at the hearth and gives her the first fruits at Chasok Tangnam before the new grain may be eaten; the Rai honour Paruhang and Sumnima in the biannual Sakela festivals (Ubhauli and Udhauli), whose sili dance-steps mimic animals and the labour of cultivation.
dossier.canon: The Mundhum itself (Rai muddum/ridum) — an oral scripture whose authority lives in correct recitation, not reading; parts were put in writing in I.S. Chemjong's 20th-century compilations, and the Limbu Sirijunga script, revived by the martyred teacher Sirijunga (1704-1741), serves as its written vehicle.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds scripture as performed itinerary — a canon that is simultaneously map, pedigree and rite, where reciting the route of the ancestors is the efficacious act and the people's remembered history doubles as its liturgy.
sources: I.S. Chemjong, 'History and Culture of the Kirat People' (1966)
Martin Gaenszle, 'Origins and Migrations: Kinship, Mythology and Ethnic Identity among the Mewahang Rai of East Nepal' (2000)
Philippe Sagant, 'The Dozing Shaman: The Limbus of Eastern Nepal' (1996)
J.R. Subba, 'Philosophy and Teachings of Yuma Samyo (Yumaism)' (1998)

links.cite: J.R. Subba, 'Philosophy and Teachings of Yuma Samyo (Yumaism)' (1998)
